Hello! Hello, this is a coding assessment. I would prefer you read the PDF, which is engineering impact, and then go through it.

I need you to build the application for me. I have written a rough plan in the plan folder, where I've also described a few metrics and how I visualize the architecture for the system. Where you come in is figuring out how to implement the first two parts of it, like gathering data and then also building a robust evaluator, which makes sense. We're just getting the scores from the LLM but you are the one who probably will have to brainstorm some metrics and how to make it robust and verifiable. That's one of the requirements from the assignment: the results need to be verifiable if needed. Even though we are using an LLM, we need the LLM responses to make sense so we can possibly generate a few metrics which the LLM can rate. Essentially I can eval where the LLM will give out scores and then we use those scores to create our own data. We can show that on the front end but yeah that's pretty much it.

Go through the plan. I have talked very extensively about what this task is there and also the ideas. Read the engineering doc first then read my plan and then let's brainstorm about the architecture and just score quality metrics.  ultrathink

# Scribble for my initial thoughts / Claude first input

# Written using voice dictation tool, ignore punctuations and grammar.

So, yeah, based on the document and the coding assessment, the goal is to identify top contributors, top engineers who contribute the "best" for the repository. Please go through the "engineering impact assignment document" before evaluating my prompt/task. 

The first things that come to my mind are:
- basic counts and code contributions
- PRs, commits, lines of code, maybe file types
Then maybe generating a composite score for them, assigning weights to each contribution, creating a few graphs, and identifying top contributors that way. We can take it a bit further by also classifying different contributions and considering them. I don't believe in counts; I don't think the number of PRs, commits, and lines of code matter that much. The type of contribution they are making is also important, so just pure PRs, commits, and lines of code I'll probably skip, but I'll consider the counts of actual impactful contributions.

The next thing is, how do we identify an important, impactful contribution, right? 

These can be classified by what exactly is the contribution, by checking if it's:
- the actual code
- just documentation
- just configuration
- updating libraries
- something in the code, like a feature or an actual bug fix, contributing to something that is being fixed, which is high impact
We can have an LLM judge that. If it's a new feature, then obviously it's a huge impact, especially if it's a feature integrating with the existing system. I would prioritize code, backend, a lot more than the docs, configurations, updating libraries, and even minor bug fixes, which are not that important, like adding type constraints, which are potentially handled during runtime already by the code and just get added for code quality. That should not be a huge contribution.

Next, I would say, is the review quality as well. For me, a good engineer is also one who unblocks others as fast as possible and adds comments. Just approval, just looking at an engineer who responds the fastest to a particular issue, can be a minor metric as well, which is essentially an unblock rate for someone. Also, for the contributor, it can be how quickly they incorporate review comments in their code, if they are ignoring it, if they have never been worked upon. All those metrics can also go in the impact score. So essentially, a signal where PRs are, if someone has raised a PR and there are comments on it, how quickly they incorporate the feedback. 

Next can be something like purely technical: how many parts of the code they touch. If they cover all parts of it, like:
- front end
- back end
- side features
- database
- everything
Or if it's just one particular system that they're focusing on, like a more generalist engineer is more valuable than a specialist. Again, that is debatable. If the specialist engineer is essentially taking care of the entire feature, he is probably more important than a generalist who doesn't own anything. That is also one part of it.

So these are the thoughts I have. I do want to make it like this: I don't believe in static weighted scores. I would prefer for an LLM to take this over first for the implementation and thinking of data collection, and then we can build a script or system which generates the scores for the data and prepares the results. This can be our actual statistics that we want to use.  
  
There is a separate application or the front end part which displays the output that is saved in the same file and then uses it on the front page to show the statistics. That's the architecture that I'm thinking. Essentially, we are doing two things:

1. A Python or backend-based evaluator which possibly can read input and give out output.
2. Input/Data gatherer: how to fetch the input data from github, I don't know, GitHub APIs or scraper. I will leave that upto youl. We can target data from the last 30 days to keep it simple. And the final output: we can save these statistics somewhere and then evaluate Python-based again. I guess I'm open to it, which possibly has an evaluator that can generate an output file which are essentially statistics for the data that we have. We can use an algorithm behind it, or I'm okay creating an algorithm, but I would prefer not to do that just to have something more robust. I think having an LLM judge is better, so that's one.

The third part, or the last part, is a front end which uses the output from the evaluator and shows the statistics on the main page, which will deploy it on Vercel. 

So this is what I'm thinking. Let's chat. 

Architecture: 
1 Python for Data scraping and evaluation
2 Frontend next.js to show data on vercel deployment

Keep it simple. Don't overcomplicate it. Follow the assignments instructions as well (things llike frontend page is 1 page only, clear understandable metrics shown, fast loading UI (Fast loading is not a problem with us as we're using downloaded data)

content.text: I've read both documents. Let me now launch a Plan agent to design the evaluation architecture, then we'll discuss.

input.prompt:
I'm building an Engineering Impact Dashboard for the PostHog GitHub repository. The core challenge: identify top 5 most impactful engineers over the last 90 days.

Key constraint: Simple counts (LOC, commits, PRs) are explicitly NOT what they want. They want creative analysis. Scores must be verifiable — "Score: 207" with no context is a red flag.

The architecture is:
1. Python data gatherer (GitHub API → raw data JSON)
2. Python LLM evaluator (raw data → scored/analyzed JSON)
3. Next.js frontend (scored JSON → single-page dashboard on Vercel)

I need you to deeply think through the **LLM evaluation layer** — this is the differentiator. Consider:

**A. What dimensions should each PR be scored on by the LLM?**
Think about: complexity, scope of impact, type classification (feature/bugfix/refactor/docs/config/tests/dependencies), risk level, novelty. Each score 1-10.

**B. What dimensions should each code review be scored on?**
Think about: review depth (rubber stamp vs substantive), whether it caught real issues, constructiveness.

**C. How do we aggregate per-engineer scores from PR-level and review-level scores?**
Design the formula. Consider: contribution impact, review impact, collaboration velocity (from timestamps), codebase breadth (from file paths), feedback responsiveness.

**D. How do we make this verifiable?**
The audience is a busy engineering leader. They should be able to:
- See what each sub-score means
- Click into a specific PR to check if the LLM's score makes sense
- Understand the formula

**E. Practical concerns:**
- PostHog is very active (~10-30 PRs merged/day). 90 days = potentially 1000+ PRs.
- We should use GitHub GraphQL API for efficiency.
- LLM calls should be batched (5-10 PRs per call) for speed.
- Which model? Claude Haiku for speed/cost, or Sonnet for accuracy?
- What goes into each LLM prompt? We can't send full diffs (too large). Send: title, description, file names changed, diff stats, labels.

**F. Edge cases:**
- Bot accounts (dependabot, github-actions) must be excluded
- Some PRs are trivial (typo fixes, version bumps) — the scoring should naturally handle this
- Some engineers might only review, not author PRs — they should still get credit

Design the complete evaluation architecture. Be specific about prompt structure, scoring rubrics, aggregation formula, and how the frontend should display this for verifiability.

Do NOT write any code. Just design the system.

content.text:
Good. Now I have full context on the project. This is a greenfield project -- no code written yet, just the assignment PDF and the initial thoughts document. Let me design the complete LLM evaluation architecture.

---

# Complete LLM Evaluation Architecture for the Engineering Impact Dashboard

## Part 1: Philosophy -- What "Impact" Means

Before any scoring rubric, the system needs a clear, defensible definition of impact. Here is the one I recommend, which aligns with your initial thoughts:

**Impact = the degree to which an engineer moves the product forward and makes the team around them more effective.**

This decomposes into two pillars:
1. **Direct Contribution Impact** -- the value of the code they authored and shipped
2. **Multiplier Impact** -- how they accelerated others through reviews, unblocking, and collaboration

This definition naturally handles the assignment's core constraint: a typo-fix PR gets a low contribution-impact score even if it touches 50 files, while a single well-designed feature PR or a review that catches a critical bug scores high.

---

## Part 2: Data Gathering Layer (what the LLM receives)

### 2A. GitHub GraphQL Query Strategy

Use the GitHub GraphQL API to fetch merged PRs from `PostHog/posthog` in the last 90 days. A single paginated query can pull 100 PRs per page. For ~1000-2700 PRs over 90 days, this means 10-27 API calls -- well within rate limits.

**Per PR, fetch:**
- `title`, `body` (PR description, truncated to first 1500 chars)
- `author.login`
- `labels.nodes[].name`
- `mergedAt`, `createdAt`
- `additions`, `deletions`, `changedFiles`
- `files.nodes[].path` (first 50 files -- enough to determine scope)
- `reviews.nodes[]` containing: `author.login`, `state` (APPROVED / CHANGES_REQUESTED / COMMENTED), `body` (truncated to 500 chars), `submittedAt`
- `comments.totalCount` (discussion volume)
- `reviewRequests.nodes[].requestedReviewer.login`

**Per review comment thread** (separate query, batched):
- `body` of top-level review comments (not inline -- those are too granular)
- Whether the review led to changes (heuristic: CHANGES_REQUESTED followed by new commits before merge)

### 2B. Bot Filtering

Before any LLM call, programmatically exclude:
- Accounts matching: `dependabot`, `github-actions`, `posthog-bot`, `codecov`, any login containing `[bot]`
- PRs with labels like `dependencies`, `automated` (if PostHog uses them)

### 2C. Data Shape Sent to LLM

For each batch of 5-8 PRs, construct a prompt containing a structured summary per PR. The LLM never sees raw diffs -- it sees:

```
PR #12345: "Add cohort-based feature flags"
Author: engineer_a
Labels: [feature, backend]
Description: "This PR adds the ability to target feature flags at cohorts
  defined by behavioral properties. It introduces a new CohortMatcher
  class and integrates with the existing flag evaluation pipeline..."
Files changed (38): 
  - posthog/api/feature_flag.py
  - posthog/models/cohort/matcher.py (new)
  - posthog/models/cohort/test_matcher.py (new)
  - ee/clickhouse/queries/feature_flags.py
  - frontend/src/scenes/feature-flags/FeatureFlagForm.tsx
  - ... (33 more, spanning posthog/api/, posthog/models/, ee/clickhouse/, frontend/)
Stats: +847 / -123 across 38 files
Time open: 4 days | Discussion comments: 12
Reviews:
  - engineer_b: CHANGES_REQUESTED - "The CohortMatcher should handle 
    the case where a cohort references another cohort recursively..."
  - engineer_c: APPROVED - "LGTM"
  - engineer_b: APPROVED (after changes) - "Recursive case handled well now"
```

---

## Part 3: PR-Level LLM Scoring (Contribution Impact)

### 3A. Scoring Dimensions (each 1-10)

The LLM evaluates each PR on five dimensions:

| Dimension | What It Measures | 1 (Low) | 5 (Medium) | 10 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Complexity** | Technical difficulty of the change | Typo fix, config tweak | Moderate logic change, adding a new API endpoint | Architectural change, new subsystem, complex algorithm |
| **Scope of Impact** | How much of the product/user base this affects | Internal tooling tweak | Single feature improvement | Core pipeline, affects all users or all data |
| **Type Weight** | Intrinsic value of the contribution category | Dependency bump, formatting | Docs, test improvements, minor bugfix | New feature, critical bugfix, performance optimization, security fix |
| **Risk & Judgment** | Level of engineering judgment required | Safe, isolated change | Moderate risk, touches shared code | High-stakes migration, backward-compat concerns, data integrity |
| **Novelty** | Is this new capability vs. maintenance? | Routine maintenance | Enhancement of existing capability | Net-new capability or significant rethinking of approach |

### 3B. Type Classification

The LLM also classifies each PR into exactly one type:
- `feature` -- new user-facing or developer-facing capability
- `bugfix-critical` -- fixes data loss, crashes, security vulnerabilities
- `bugfix-minor` -- cosmetic fixes, edge cases
- `refactor` -- restructuring without behavior change
- `performance` -- speed/resource optimization
- `tests` -- adding/improving test coverage
- `docs` -- documentation only
- `config/infra` -- CI, build config, deployment
- `dependencies` -- library updates

### 3C. PR Composite Score Formula

```
pr_contribution_score = (
    complexity * 0.25 +
    scope_of_impact * 0.30 +
    type_weight * 0.20 +
    risk_and_judgment * 0.15 +
    novelty * 0.10
)
```

This yields a 1-10 float per PR. The weights emphasize scope (does it matter?) and complexity (was it hard?) over novelty (is it new?).

### 3D. LLM Prompt Structure for PR Scoring

```
You are an engineering impact evaluator. You will analyze a batch of 
Pull Requests from the PostHog open-source repository and score each one.

For EACH PR below, provide:
1. type_classification: one of [feature, bugfix-critical, bugfix-minor, 
   refactor, performance, tests, docs, config/infra, dependencies]
2. complexity (1-10): How technically difficult was this change?
   1=trivial (typo, version bump) 5=moderate (new endpoint, logic change) 
   10=architectural (new subsystem, complex algorithm, data migration)
3. scope_of_impact (1-10): How broadly does this affect the product?
   1=internal/cosmetic 5=single feature 10=core pipeline, affects all users
4. type_weight (1-10): How inherently valuable is this type of work?
   1=dependency bump 3=docs/config 5=tests/minor bugfix 7=refactor/perf 
   9=feature 10=critical bugfix/security
5. risk_and_judgment (1-10): How much engineering judgment was needed?
   1=safe, isolated 5=moderate shared-code risk 10=migration, data integrity
6. novelty (1-10): New capability vs. maintenance?
   1=routine 5=enhancement 10=net-new capability
7. one_line_summary: A single sentence explaining what this PR does and 
   why it matters (this will be shown to users on the dashboard)
8. reasoning: 2-3 sentences justifying your scores (this is for 
   verifiability -- an engineering leader should be able to read this 
   and say "yes, that makes sense")

Respond in JSON format as an array of objects, one per PR.

=== PRs to evaluate ===
{batch of 5-8 PR summaries as shown in Part 2C}
```

### 3E. Why Batching at 5-8 PRs

- Fewer than 5: too many API calls, slow, expensive
- More than 10: the LLM loses attention on later PRs in the batch, quality degrades
- 5-8 is the sweet spot: ~150 LLM calls for 1000 PRs, finishes in 5-10 minutes with parallelism

---

## Part 4: Review-Level LLM Scoring (Multiplier Impact)

### 4A. Scoring Dimensions (each 1-10)

For each review (not each PR -- one person may review the same PR multiple times), the LLM scores:

| Dimension | What It Measures | 1 (Low) | 5 (Medium) | 10 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Review Depth** | Substantiveness of the review | "LGTM" / rubber stamp | Specific comments on logic | Identifies architectural issues, suggests alternatives |
| **Issue Detection** | Did the review catch real problems? | No issues raised | Minor style/naming issues | Caught bugs, race conditions, security issues |
| **Constructiveness** | Did the review help the author improve? | No actionable feedback | Pointed out problems | Pointed out problems AND suggested solutions |

### 4B. Review Composite Score

```
review_score = (
    review_depth * 0.40 +
    issue_detection * 0.35 +
    constructiveness * 0.25
)
```

### 4C. Review Context Amplifier

A review on a high-impact PR matters more than a review on a trivial PR. So the final review impact is:

```
weighted_review_score = review_score * (pr_contribution_score / 10)
```

This means reviewing a 9/10 PR deeply is worth 9x more than rubber-stamping a 1/10 PR.

### 4D. LLM Prompt for Review Scoring

```
You are evaluating the quality of code reviews on the PostHog repository.

For each review below, score:
1. review_depth (1-10): How substantive was this review?
   1="LGTM"/rubber stamp  5=specific comments on logic  
   10=identifies architectural issues, suggests alternatives
2. issue_detection (1-10): Did the reviewer catch real problems?
   1=no issues raised  5=style/naming issues  10=bugs, race conditions, security
3. constructiveness (1-10): Did the review help the author improve?
   1=no actionable feedback  5=pointed out problems  
   10=pointed out problems AND suggested solutions
4. reasoning: 1-2 sentences explaining your assessment

Context about the PR being reviewed is provided so you can assess 
whether the review was appropriate for the PR's complexity.

=== Reviews to evaluate ===
{batch of reviews with their parent PR context}
```

### 4E. Handling "Approval-Only" Reviews

Many reviews are just approvals with no comment body. The LLM should score these as:
- `review_depth: 2` (not 1 -- they did look at the code)
- `issue_detection: 1`
- `constructiveness: 1`
- `reasoning: "Approval with no substantive comments"`

This can be handled programmatically to save LLM calls: if `review.state == "APPROVED"` and `review.body` is empty or just "LGTM", assign these default scores without calling the LLM.

---

## Part 5: Per-Engineer Aggregation Formula

### 5A. Four Impact Pillars

For each engineer, compute four pillar scores:

**Pillar 1: Contribution Impact (40% of final score)**
```
contribution_impact = sum(pr_contribution_score for each authored PR) 
                      / max_contribution_across_all_engineers * 100
```
Normalized to 0-100 so the top contributor gets 100. This is NOT a count -- an engineer with 5 high-impact PRs (scores: 9, 8, 9, 7, 8 = 41) outranks one with 20 trivial PRs (scores: 2, 1, 2, 1, 1... = 25).

**Pillar 2: Review Impact (25% of final score)**
```
review_impact = sum(weighted_review_score for each review given)
                / max_review_across_all_engineers * 100
```
Same normalization. An engineer who only reviews (no authored PRs) can still score high here.

**Pillar 3: Collaboration Velocity (20% of final score)**

This measures two things:
- **Review turnaround**: Median time from when a review was requested to when the engineer submitted their review. Faster = better. Score: `100 * (1 - clamp(median_hours / 48, 0, 1))`. Under 2 hours = ~96, under 24 hours = ~50, over 48 hours = 0.
- **Feedback incorporation**: For authored PRs that received CHANGES_REQUESTED, median time from that event to the next commit. Same formula as above.

```
collaboration_velocity = (review_turnaround * 0.6 + feedback_incorporation * 0.4)
```

**Pillar 4: Codebase Breadth (15% of final score)**

Computed deterministically (no LLM needed). From the file paths touched across all PRs:

Define PostHog codebase zones:
- `frontend/` -- Frontend (React/TypeScript)
- `posthog/api/` -- Backend API
- `posthog/models/` -- Data models
- `ee/clickhouse/` -- Analytics engine
- `plugin-server/` -- Plugin system
- `posthog/tasks/` -- Background tasks
- `posthog/management/` -- Django management
- `cypress/` or `playwright/` -- E2E tests
- Other zones as discovered

```
breadth_score = (unique_zones_touched / total_zones) * 100
```

An engineer touching 6 of 8 zones scores 75. This rewards full-stack contributors without penalizing specialists unfairly (the weight is only 15%).

### 5B. Final Composite Score

```
final_score = (
    contribution_impact * 0.40 +
    review_impact * 0.25 +
    collaboration_velocity * 0.20 +
    codebase_breadth * 0.15
)
```

This is a 0-100 score. Crucially, the dashboard never shows just this number. It shows the four pillars and their sub-components.

### 5C. Why These Weights

- **40% contribution**: The assignment is about "most impactful engineers" -- what they build matters most.
- **25% review**: Your initial thoughts emphasized unblocking others. This is a significant weight, meaning pure reviewers can rank top-5.
- **20% velocity**: Fast unblocking is a force multiplier. This rewards responsiveness without rewarding sloppy speed (the review quality scores handle that).
- **15% breadth**: A tiebreaker. Generalists get a modest bonus. Specialists are not punished because the weight is low.

---

## Part 6: Model Choice Decision

**Recommendation: Claude 3.5 Haiku for PR scoring, with Sonnet as a fallback for ambiguous cases.**

Reasoning:
- The scoring rubric is highly structured (five numeric fields + classification + short text). Haiku handles structured-output tasks well.
- At ~1000 PRs in batches of 6, you need ~167 LLM calls. Haiku processes these in ~3-5 minutes with parallelism and costs ~$0.50-1.00 total.
- Sonnet would cost ~5x more and take ~3x longer, with marginal accuracy improvement on a structured rubric.
- The rubric itself constrains the LLM's judgment space enough that model differences are small.

However, if you observe quality issues (e.g., all PRs getting scores of 5-7 with no differentiation), switch to Sonnet. The prompt and schema are identical.

For review scoring, the same applies: Haiku with the structured rubric.

---

## Part 7: Verifiability Architecture

This is the most critical design element given the assignment's red flags section ("Score: 207 with no context").

### 7A. Output JSON Structure

The evaluator produces a single JSON file with this structure:

```json
{
  "generated_at": "2026-03-22T10:00:00Z",
  "period": { "start": "2025-12-22", "end": "2026-03-22" },
  "methodology": {
    "description": "Impact scored across 4 pillars: Contribution (40%), Review (25%), Velocity (20%), Breadth (15%)",
    "pr_scoring": "Each PR scored by LLM on 5 dimensions: complexity, scope, type-weight, risk, novelty (1-10 each)",
    "review_scoring": "Each review scored on depth, issue-detection, constructiveness (1-10 each), weighted by parent PR impact",
    "model_used": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    "total_prs_analyzed": 1247,
    "total_reviews_analyzed": 3891,
    "bot_accounts_excluded": ["dependabot", "github-actions[bot]", ...]
  },
  "engineers": [
    {
      "rank": 1,
      "login": "engineer_a",
      "avatar_url": "https://...",
      "final_score": 87.3,
      "pillars": {
        "contribution_impact": { "score": 92.1, "weight": 0.40, "pr_count": 34, "top_pr_type": "feature" },
        "review_impact": { "score": 78.5, "weight": 0.25, "review_count": 67 },
        "collaboration_velocity": { 
          "score": 85.0, "weight": 0.20,
          "median_review_turnaround_hours": 3.2,
          "median_feedback_incorporation_hours": 5.1
        },
        "codebase_breadth": { "score": 87.5, "weight": 0.15, "zones_touched": 7, "zones_total": 8 }
      },
      "highlight_prs": [
        {
          "number": 12345,
          "title": "Add cohort-based feature flags",
          "url": "https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/12345",
          "type_classification": "feature",
          "scores": {
            "complexity": 8, "scope_of_impact": 9, "type_weight": 9,
            "risk_and_judgment": 7, "novelty": 8
          },
          "composite": 8.45,
          "one_line_summary": "Adds ability to target feature flags at behavioral cohorts, integrating with the existing flag evaluation pipeline",
          "reasoning": "High complexity: introduces CohortMatcher with recursive cohort resolution. Broad scope: affects all feature flag evaluations. Required careful judgment around backward compatibility with existing flag definitions."
        }
      ],
      "highlight_reviews": [
        {
          "pr_number": 12400,
          "pr_title": "Refactor event processing pipeline",
          "url": "https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/12400",
          "scores": { "review_depth": 9, "issue_detection": 8, "constructiveness": 9 },
          "composite": 8.75,
          "reasoning": "Identified a race condition in the batch processor and suggested a specific locking strategy"
        }
      ],
      "top_zones": ["posthog/api/", "ee/clickhouse/", "frontend/src/scenes/", "posthog/models/"]
    }
  ],
  "all_pr_scores": [
    {
      "number": 12345,
      "author": "engineer_a",
      "title": "...",
      "type_classification": "feature",
      "scores": { "complexity": 8, "scope_of_impact": 9, ... },
      "composite": 8.45,
      "one_line_summary": "...",
      "reasoning": "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

### 7B. Frontend Verifiability Design

The dashboard should show, for each top-5 engineer:

1. **The four pillar scores as a radar/bar chart** -- immediately communicates their profile shape (are they a builder? a reviewer? a generalist?)

2. **Pillar drill-down on click**: clicking "Contribution Impact: 92.1" expands to show the top 5 PRs by composite score, each with:
   - PR title (linked to GitHub)
   - Type badge (feature / bugfix / refactor)
   - The five dimension scores as small bars or numbers
   - The one-line summary
   - The reasoning text

3. **Score tooltip**: hovering over any number shows the formula. For example, hovering over the final score shows: `92.1 * 0.40 + 78.5 * 0.25 + 85.0 * 0.20 + 87.5 * 0.15 = 87.3`

4. **GitHub links everywhere**: every PR title and review links directly to the GitHub PR page. The engineering leader can click through and verify the LLM's assessment in 10 seconds.

5. **Methodology section**: a collapsible footer explaining the scoring approach in plain English, including the formula and dimension definitions.

---

## Part 8: Edge Cases and Robustness

### 8A. Trivial PRs (Natural Handling)

The scoring rubric naturally handles this. A version bump PR:
- complexity: 1
- scope_of_impact: 1
- type_weight: 1
- risk_and_judgment: 1
- novelty: 1
- composite: 1.0

This contributes almost nothing to the engineer's contribution_impact. No special filtering needed.

### 8B. Review-Only Engineers

The aggregation formula gives reviews 25% weight and velocity 20%. An engineer who reviews 100 PRs deeply with fast turnaround but authors zero PRs would get:
- contribution_impact: 0
- review_impact: up to 100
- collaboration_velocity: up to 100
- codebase_breadth: 0 (only counts authored PRs)

Max possible score: 0 + 25 + 20 + 0 = 45. This is enough to appear in top 5 if their review quality is exceptional and others are spread thin. If this feels too low, codebase_breadth could also count reviewed PR file paths, boosting the max to 60.

### 8C. Very Large PRs (Generated Code, Migrations)

Some PRs have +10,000 lines but are auto-generated schema migrations or vendor file updates. The LLM prompt should include this instruction:

```
Note: High line counts do not automatically mean high complexity. 
Auto-generated migrations, vendor file updates, and schema dumps 
should be scored low on complexity and novelty even if they add 
thousands of lines.
```

### 8D. PR Descriptions That Are Empty

Some engineers write no PR description. The LLM still has the title, file paths, and diff stats. The prompt handles this:

```
If a PR description is missing, score based on the title, file paths, 
and diff statistics. Note in your reasoning that the assessment is 
based on limited information.
```

### 8E. Co-authored PRs

GitHub shows co-authors in the commit trailer. The GraphQL API surfaces the PR author only. For this 1.5-hour scope, attribute the PR to the merge author only. Note this limitation in methodology.

---

## Part 9: Execution Pipeline

### Step 1: Data Gathering (Python script, ~5 min runtime)
```
fetch_prs(repo="PostHog/posthog", days=90)
  -> for each page of 100 merged PRs:
       -> fetch PR metadata, files, reviews via GraphQL
       -> filter out bot authors
       -> save to raw_data.json
```

### Step 2: LLM Evaluation (Python script, ~5-10 min runtime)
```
load raw_data.json
batch PRs into groups of 6

# Phase 1: Score PRs
for each batch:
    -> send to Haiku with PR scoring prompt
    -> parse JSON response
    -> validate all fields present and scores in 1-10
    -> retry on parse errors (max 2 retries)
    
# Phase 2: Score Reviews (only non-trivial reviews)
filter reviews where body is not empty and not just "LGTM"
batch reviews into groups of 8
for each batch:
    -> send to Haiku with review scoring prompt
    -> parse, validate, retry

# Phase 3: Compute per-engineer aggregates
for each unique author:
    -> compute contribution_impact (normalized)
    -> compute review_impact (normalized, weighted by PR score)
    -> compute collaboration_velocity (from timestamps)
    -> compute codebase_breadth (from file paths)
    -> compute final_score
    -> select highlight PRs and reviews

# Phase 4: Output
rank engineers by final_score
take top 5
write scored_data.json (full structure from Part 7A)
```

### Step 3: Frontend Consumes scored_data.json
The Next.js app imports the JSON at build time. No runtime API calls. Instant loading.

---

## Part 10: Cost and Performance Estimates

| Item | Count | Model | Est. Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR scoring calls | ~170 batches (1000 PRs / 6) | Haiku | ~$0.80 |
| Review scoring calls | ~100 batches (non-trivial reviews) | Haiku | ~$0.40 |
| **Total** | **~270 LLM calls** | | **~$1.20** |

Wall-clock time with 10 concurrent requests: ~3-5 minutes for the LLM evaluation phase.

If using Sonnet instead: ~$6.00 total, ~10-15 minutes. Not justified for structured rubric scoring.

---

## Part 11: What Makes This Design Stand Out

1. **Every score is decomposable.** The engineering leader sees "87.3" and can click to see 40% from contributions (92.1), 25% from reviews (78.5), etc. Each of those decomposes further into individual PR scores with reasoning text.

2. **Every score is verifiable.** Every PR and review links to GitHub. The LLM's reasoning is shown alongside the score. The leader can spot-check in seconds.

3. **The formula is transparent.** The weights are documented. The methodology section explains everything in plain English.

4. **It rewards what actually matters.** An engineer who ships one transformative feature and reviews 20 PRs thoroughly outranks an engineer who makes 50 trivial commits. The scoring rubric makes this happen naturally, not through arbitrary filtering.

5. **Review-only contributors are recognized.** This is unusual and demonstrates that impact is not just about writing code.

6. **Collaboration velocity captures the "invisible" work.** Fast review turnaround is a force multiplier that traditional metrics miss entirely.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\Plan\First Prompt.md` - Contains the original architectural thinking and constraints that the evaluation layer must align with
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\Engineering Impact Dashboard Assignment.pdf` - The source requirements document defining evaluation criteria, red flags, and the "verifiability" constraint that drives the entire output schema design
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\CLAUDE.md` - Project conventions (Python default, minimal dependencies, working > perfect) that constrain implementation choices
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\.claude\settings.local.json` - Permitted toolchain (Python, pip, npm/pnpm, node) confirming the Python evaluator + Next.js frontend architecture is supported
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\.claude\skills\api\SKILL.md` - Pattern for building API-style services if the evaluator needs to expose results via an endpoint rather than static JSON

toolUseResult.prompt:
I'm building an Engineering Impact Dashboard for the PostHog GitHub repository. The core challenge: identify top 5 most impactful engineers over the last 90 days.

Key constraint: Simple counts (LOC, commits, PRs) are explicitly NOT what they want. They want creative analysis. Scores must be verifiable — "Score: 207" with no context is a red flag.

The architecture is:
1. Python data gatherer (GitHub API → raw data JSON)
2. Python LLM evaluator (raw data → scored/analyzed JSON)
3. Next.js frontend (scored JSON → single-page dashboard on Vercel)

I need you to deeply think through the **LLM evaluation layer** — this is the differentiator. Consider:

**A. What dimensions should each PR be scored on by the LLM?**
Think about: complexity, scope of impact, type classification (feature/bugfix/refactor/docs/config/tests/dependencies), risk level, novelty. Each score 1-10.

**B. What dimensions should each code review be scored on?**
Think about: review depth (rubber stamp vs substantive), whether it caught real issues, constructiveness.

**C. How do we aggregate per-engineer scores from PR-level and review-level scores?**
Design the formula. Consider: contribution impact, review impact, collaboration velocity (from timestamps), codebase breadth (from file paths), feedback responsiveness.

**D. How do we make this verifiable?**
The audience is a busy engineering leader. They should be able to:
- See what each sub-score means
- Click into a specific PR to check if the LLM's score makes sense
- Understand the formula

**E. Practical concerns:**
- PostHog is very active (~10-30 PRs merged/day). 90 days = potentially 1000+ PRs.
- We should use GitHub GraphQL API for efficiency.
- LLM calls should be batched (5-10 PRs per call) for speed.
- Which model? Claude Haiku for speed/cost, or Sonnet for accuracy?
- What goes into each LLM prompt? We can't send full diffs (too large). Send: title, description, file names changed, diff stats, labels.

**F. Edge cases:**
- Bot accounts (dependabot, github-actions) must be excluded
- Some PRs are trivial (typo fixes, version bumps) — the scoring should naturally handle this
- Some engineers might only review, not author PRs — they should still get credit

Design the complete evaluation architecture. Be specific about prompt structure, scoring rubrics, aggregation formula, and how the frontend should display this for verifiability.

Do NOT write any code. Just design the system.

content.text:
Good. Now I have full context on the project. This is a greenfield project -- no code written yet, just the assignment PDF and the initial thoughts document. Let me design the complete LLM evaluation architecture.

---

# Complete LLM Evaluation Architecture for the Engineering Impact Dashboard

## Part 1: Philosophy -- What "Impact" Means

Before any scoring rubric, the system needs a clear, defensible definition of impact. Here is the one I recommend, which aligns with your initial thoughts:

**Impact = the degree to which an engineer moves the product forward and makes the team around them more effective.**

This decomposes into two pillars:
1. **Direct Contribution Impact** -- the value of the code they authored and shipped
2. **Multiplier Impact** -- how they accelerated others through reviews, unblocking, and collaboration

This definition naturally handles the assignment's core constraint: a typo-fix PR gets a low contribution-impact score even if it touches 50 files, while a single well-designed feature PR or a review that catches a critical bug scores high.

---

## Part 2: Data Gathering Layer (what the LLM receives)

### 2A. GitHub GraphQL Query Strategy

Use the GitHub GraphQL API to fetch merged PRs from `PostHog/posthog` in the last 90 days. A single paginated query can pull 100 PRs per page. For ~1000-2700 PRs over 90 days, this means 10-27 API calls -- well within rate limits.

**Per PR, fetch:**
- `title`, `body` (PR description, truncated to first 1500 chars)
- `author.login`
- `labels.nodes[].name`
- `mergedAt`, `createdAt`
- `additions`, `deletions`, `changedFiles`
- `files.nodes[].path` (first 50 files -- enough to determine scope)
- `reviews.nodes[]` containing: `author.login`, `state` (APPROVED / CHANGES_REQUESTED / COMMENTED), `body` (truncated to 500 chars), `submittedAt`
- `comments.totalCount` (discussion volume)
- `reviewRequests.nodes[].requestedReviewer.login`

**Per review comment thread** (separate query, batched):
- `body` of top-level review comments (not inline -- those are too granular)
- Whether the review led to changes (heuristic: CHANGES_REQUESTED followed by new commits before merge)

### 2B. Bot Filtering

Before any LLM call, programmatically exclude:
- Accounts matching: `dependabot`, `github-actions`, `posthog-bot`, `codecov`, any login containing `[bot]`
- PRs with labels like `dependencies`, `automated` (if PostHog uses them)

### 2C. Data Shape Sent to LLM

For each batch of 5-8 PRs, construct a prompt containing a structured summary per PR. The LLM never sees raw diffs -- it sees:

```
PR #12345: "Add cohort-based feature flags"
Author: engineer_a
Labels: [feature, backend]
Description: "This PR adds the ability to target feature flags at cohorts
  defined by behavioral properties. It introduces a new CohortMatcher
  class and integrates with the existing flag evaluation pipeline..."
Files changed (38): 
  - posthog/api/feature_flag.py
  - posthog/models/cohort/matcher.py (new)
  - posthog/models/cohort/test_matcher.py (new)
  - ee/clickhouse/queries/feature_flags.py
  - frontend/src/scenes/feature-flags/FeatureFlagForm.tsx
  - ... (33 more, spanning posthog/api/, posthog/models/, ee/clickhouse/, frontend/)
Stats: +847 / -123 across 38 files
Time open: 4 days | Discussion comments: 12
Reviews:
  - engineer_b: CHANGES_REQUESTED - "The CohortMatcher should handle 
    the case where a cohort references another cohort recursively..."
  - engineer_c: APPROVED - "LGTM"
  - engineer_b: APPROVED (after changes) - "Recursive case handled well now"
```

---

## Part 3: PR-Level LLM Scoring (Contribution Impact)

### 3A. Scoring Dimensions (each 1-10)

The LLM evaluates each PR on five dimensions:

| Dimension | What It Measures | 1 (Low) | 5 (Medium) | 10 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Complexity** | Technical difficulty of the change | Typo fix, config tweak | Moderate logic change, adding a new API endpoint | Architectural change, new subsystem, complex algorithm |
| **Scope of Impact** | How much of the product/user base this affects | Internal tooling tweak | Single feature improvement | Core pipeline, affects all users or all data |
| **Type Weight** | Intrinsic value of the contribution category | Dependency bump, formatting | Docs, test improvements, minor bugfix | New feature, critical bugfix, performance optimization, security fix |
| **Risk & Judgment** | Level of engineering judgment required | Safe, isolated change | Moderate risk, touches shared code | High-stakes migration, backward-compat concerns, data integrity |
| **Novelty** | Is this new capability vs. maintenance? | Routine maintenance | Enhancement of existing capability | Net-new capability or significant rethinking of approach |

### 3B. Type Classification

The LLM also classifies each PR into exactly one type:
- `feature` -- new user-facing or developer-facing capability
- `bugfix-critical` -- fixes data loss, crashes, security vulnerabilities
- `bugfix-minor` -- cosmetic fixes, edge cases
- `refactor` -- restructuring without behavior change
- `performance` -- speed/resource optimization
- `tests` -- adding/improving test coverage
- `docs` -- documentation only
- `config/infra` -- CI, build config, deployment
- `dependencies` -- library updates

### 3C. PR Composite Score Formula

```
pr_contribution_score = (
    complexity * 0.25 +
    scope_of_impact * 0.30 +
    type_weight * 0.20 +
    risk_and_judgment * 0.15 +
    novelty * 0.10
)
```

This yields a 1-10 float per PR. The weights emphasize scope (does it matter?) and complexity (was it hard?) over novelty (is it new?).

### 3D. LLM Prompt Structure for PR Scoring

```
You are an engineering impact evaluator. You will analyze a batch of 
Pull Requests from the PostHog open-source repository and score each one.

For EACH PR below, provide:
1. type_classification: one of [feature, bugfix-critical, bugfix-minor, 
   refactor, performance, tests, docs, config/infra, dependencies]
2. complexity (1-10): How technically difficult was this change?
   1=trivial (typo, version bump) 5=moderate (new endpoint, logic change) 
   10=architectural (new subsystem, complex algorithm, data migration)
3. scope_of_impact (1-10): How broadly does this affect the product?
   1=internal/cosmetic 5=single feature 10=core pipeline, affects all users
4. type_weight (1-10): How inherently valuable is this type of work?
   1=dependency bump 3=docs/config 5=tests/minor bugfix 7=refactor/perf 
   9=feature 10=critical bugfix/security
5. risk_and_judgment (1-10): How much engineering judgment was needed?
   1=safe, isolated 5=moderate shared-code risk 10=migration, data integrity
6. novelty (1-10): New capability vs. maintenance?
   1=routine 5=enhancement 10=net-new capability
7. one_line_summary: A single sentence explaining what this PR does and 
   why it matters (this will be shown to users on the dashboard)
8. reasoning: 2-3 sentences justifying your scores (this is for 
   verifiability -- an engineering leader should be able to read this 
   and say "yes, that makes sense")

Respond in JSON format as an array of objects, one per PR.

=== PRs to evaluate ===
{batch of 5-8 PR summaries as shown in Part 2C}
```

### 3E. Why Batching at 5-8 PRs

- Fewer than 5: too many API calls, slow, expensive
- More than 10: the LLM loses attention on later PRs in the batch, quality degrades
- 5-8 is the sweet spot: ~150 LLM calls for 1000 PRs, finishes in 5-10 minutes with parallelism

---

## Part 4: Review-Level LLM Scoring (Multiplier Impact)

### 4A. Scoring Dimensions (each 1-10)

For each review (not each PR -- one person may review the same PR multiple times), the LLM scores:

| Dimension | What It Measures | 1 (Low) | 5 (Medium) | 10 (High) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Review Depth** | Substantiveness of the review | "LGTM" / rubber stamp | Specific comments on logic | Identifies architectural issues, suggests alternatives |
| **Issue Detection** | Did the review catch real problems? | No issues raised | Minor style/naming issues | Caught bugs, race conditions, security issues |
| **Constructiveness** | Did the review help the author improve? | No actionable feedback | Pointed out problems | Pointed out problems AND suggested solutions |

### 4B. Review Composite Score

```
review_score = (
    review_depth * 0.40 +
    issue_detection * 0.35 +
    constructiveness * 0.25
)
```

### 4C. Review Context Amplifier

A review on a high-impact PR matters more than a review on a trivial PR. So the final review impact is:

```
weighted_review_score = review_score * (pr_contribution_score / 10)
```

This means reviewing a 9/10 PR deeply is worth 9x more than rubber-stamping a 1/10 PR.

### 4D. LLM Prompt for Review Scoring

```
You are evaluating the quality of code reviews on the PostHog repository.

For each review below, score:
1. review_depth (1-10): How substantive was this review?
   1="LGTM"/rubber stamp  5=specific comments on logic  
   10=identifies architectural issues, suggests alternatives
2. issue_detection (1-10): Did the reviewer catch real problems?
   1=no issues raised  5=style/naming issues  10=bugs, race conditions, security
3. constructiveness (1-10): Did the review help the author improve?
   1=no actionable feedback  5=pointed out problems  
   10=pointed out problems AND suggested solutions
4. reasoning: 1-2 sentences explaining your assessment

Context about the PR being reviewed is provided so you can assess 
whether the review was appropriate for the PR's complexity.

=== Reviews to evaluate ===
{batch of reviews with their parent PR context}
```

### 4E. Handling "Approval-Only" Reviews

Many reviews are just approvals with no comment body. The LLM should score these as:
- `review_depth: 2` (not 1 -- they did look at the code)
- `issue_detection: 1`
- `constructiveness: 1`
- `reasoning: "Approval with no substantive comments"`

This can be handled programmatically to save LLM calls: if `review.state == "APPROVED"` and `review.body` is empty or just "LGTM", assign these default scores without calling the LLM.

---

## Part 5: Per-Engineer Aggregation Formula

### 5A. Four Impact Pillars

For each engineer, compute four pillar scores:

**Pillar 1: Contribution Impact (40% of final score)**
```
contribution_impact = sum(pr_contribution_score for each authored PR) 
                      / max_contribution_across_all_engineers * 100
```
Normalized to 0-100 so the top contributor gets 100. This is NOT a count -- an engineer with 5 high-impact PRs (scores: 9, 8, 9, 7, 8 = 41) outranks one with 20 trivial PRs (scores: 2, 1, 2, 1, 1... = 25).

**Pillar 2: Review Impact (25% of final score)**
```
review_impact = sum(weighted_review_score for each review given)
                / max_review_across_all_engineers * 100
```
Same normalization. An engineer who only reviews (no authored PRs) can still score high here.

**Pillar 3: Collaboration Velocity (20% of final score)**

This measures two things:
- **Review turnaround**: Median time from when a review was requested to when the engineer submitted their review. Faster = better. Score: `100 * (1 - clamp(median_hours / 48, 0, 1))`. Under 2 hours = ~96, under 24 hours = ~50, over 48 hours = 0.
- **Feedback incorporation**: For authored PRs that received CHANGES_REQUESTED, median time from that event to the next commit. Same formula as above.

```
collaboration_velocity = (review_turnaround * 0.6 + feedback_incorporation * 0.4)
```

**Pillar 4: Codebase Breadth (15% of final score)**

Computed deterministically (no LLM needed). From the file paths touched across all PRs:

Define PostHog codebase zones:
- `frontend/` -- Frontend (React/TypeScript)
- `posthog/api/` -- Backend API
- `posthog/models/` -- Data models
- `ee/clickhouse/` -- Analytics engine
- `plugin-server/` -- Plugin system
- `posthog/tasks/` -- Background tasks
- `posthog/management/` -- Django management
- `cypress/` or `playwright/` -- E2E tests
- Other zones as discovered

```
breadth_score = (unique_zones_touched / total_zones) * 100
```

An engineer touching 6 of 8 zones scores 75. This rewards full-stack contributors without penalizing specialists unfairly (the weight is only 15%).

### 5B. Final Composite Score

```
final_score = (
    contribution_impact * 0.40 +
    review_impact * 0.25 +
    collaboration_velocity * 0.20 +
    codebase_breadth * 0.15
)
```

This is a 0-100 score. Crucially, the dashboard never shows just this number. It shows the four pillars and their sub-components.

### 5C. Why These Weights

- **40% contribution**: The assignment is about "most impactful engineers" -- what they build matters most.
- **25% review**: Your initial thoughts emphasized unblocking others. This is a significant weight, meaning pure reviewers can rank top-5.
- **20% velocity**: Fast unblocking is a force multiplier. This rewards responsiveness without rewarding sloppy speed (the review quality scores handle that).
- **15% breadth**: A tiebreaker. Generalists get a modest bonus. Specialists are not punished because the weight is low.

---

## Part 6: Model Choice Decision

**Recommendation: Claude 3.5 Haiku for PR scoring, with Sonnet as a fallback for ambiguous cases.**

Reasoning:
- The scoring rubric is highly structured (five numeric fields + classification + short text). Haiku handles structured-output tasks well.
- At ~1000 PRs in batches of 6, you need ~167 LLM calls. Haiku processes these in ~3-5 minutes with parallelism and costs ~$0.50-1.00 total.
- Sonnet would cost ~5x more and take ~3x longer, with marginal accuracy improvement on a structured rubric.
- The rubric itself constrains the LLM's judgment space enough that model differences are small.

However, if you observe quality issues (e.g., all PRs getting scores of 5-7 with no differentiation), switch to Sonnet. The prompt and schema are identical.

For review scoring, the same applies: Haiku with the structured rubric.

---

## Part 7: Verifiability Architecture

This is the most critical design element given the assignment's red flags section ("Score: 207 with no context").

### 7A. Output JSON Structure

The evaluator produces a single JSON file with this structure:

```json
{
  "generated_at": "2026-03-22T10:00:00Z",
  "period": { "start": "2025-12-22", "end": "2026-03-22" },
  "methodology": {
    "description": "Impact scored across 4 pillars: Contribution (40%), Review (25%), Velocity (20%), Breadth (15%)",
    "pr_scoring": "Each PR scored by LLM on 5 dimensions: complexity, scope, type-weight, risk, novelty (1-10 each)",
    "review_scoring": "Each review scored on depth, issue-detection, constructiveness (1-10 each), weighted by parent PR impact",
    "model_used": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    "total_prs_analyzed": 1247,
    "total_reviews_analyzed": 3891,
    "bot_accounts_excluded": ["dependabot", "github-actions[bot]", ...]
  },
  "engineers": [
    {
      "rank": 1,
      "login": "engineer_a",
      "avatar_url": "https://...",
      "final_score": 87.3,
      "pillars": {
        "contribution_impact": { "score": 92.1, "weight": 0.40, "pr_count": 34, "top_pr_type": "feature" },
        "review_impact": { "score": 78.5, "weight": 0.25, "review_count": 67 },
        "collaboration_velocity": { 
          "score": 85.0, "weight": 0.20,
          "median_review_turnaround_hours": 3.2,
          "median_feedback_incorporation_hours": 5.1
        },
        "codebase_breadth": { "score": 87.5, "weight": 0.15, "zones_touched": 7, "zones_total": 8 }
      },
      "highlight_prs": [
        {
          "number": 12345,
          "title": "Add cohort-based feature flags",
          "url": "https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/12345",
          "type_classification": "feature",
          "scores": {
            "complexity": 8, "scope_of_impact": 9, "type_weight": 9,
            "risk_and_judgment": 7, "novelty": 8
          },
          "composite": 8.45,
          "one_line_summary": "Adds ability to target feature flags at behavioral cohorts, integrating with the existing flag evaluation pipeline",
          "reasoning": "High complexity: introduces CohortMatcher with recursive cohort resolution. Broad scope: affects all feature flag evaluations. Required careful judgment around backward compatibility with existing flag definitions."
        }
      ],
      "highlight_reviews": [
        {
          "pr_number": 12400,
          "pr_title": "Refactor event processing pipeline",
          "url": "https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/12400",
          "scores": { "review_depth": 9, "issue_detection": 8, "constructiveness": 9 },
          "composite": 8.75,
          "reasoning": "Identified a race condition in the batch processor and suggested a specific locking strategy"
        }
      ],
      "top_zones": ["posthog/api/", "ee/clickhouse/", "frontend/src/scenes/", "posthog/models/"]
    }
  ],
  "all_pr_scores": [
    {
      "number": 12345,
      "author": "engineer_a",
      "title": "...",
      "type_classification": "feature",
      "scores": { "complexity": 8, "scope_of_impact": 9, ... },
      "composite": 8.45,
      "one_line_summary": "...",
      "reasoning": "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

### 7B. Frontend Verifiability Design

The dashboard should show, for each top-5 engineer:

1. **The four pillar scores as a radar/bar chart** -- immediately communicates their profile shape (are they a builder? a reviewer? a generalist?)

2. **Pillar drill-down on click**: clicking "Contribution Impact: 92.1" expands to show the top 5 PRs by composite score, each with:
   - PR title (linked to GitHub)
   - Type badge (feature / bugfix / refactor)
   - The five dimension scores as small bars or numbers
   - The one-line summary
   - The reasoning text

3. **Score tooltip**: hovering over any number shows the formula. For example, hovering over the final score shows: `92.1 * 0.40 + 78.5 * 0.25 + 85.0 * 0.20 + 87.5 * 0.15 = 87.3`

4. **GitHub links everywhere**: every PR title and review links directly to the GitHub PR page. The engineering leader can click through and verify the LLM's assessment in 10 seconds.

5. **Methodology section**: a collapsible footer explaining the scoring approach in plain English, including the formula and dimension definitions.

---

## Part 8: Edge Cases and Robustness

### 8A. Trivial PRs (Natural Handling)

The scoring rubric naturally handles this. A version bump PR:
- complexity: 1
- scope_of_impact: 1
- type_weight: 1
- risk_and_judgment: 1
- novelty: 1
- composite: 1.0

This contributes almost nothing to the engineer's contribution_impact. No special filtering needed.

### 8B. Review-Only Engineers

The aggregation formula gives reviews 25% weight and velocity 20%. An engineer who reviews 100 PRs deeply with fast turnaround but authors zero PRs would get:
- contribution_impact: 0
- review_impact: up to 100
- collaboration_velocity: up to 100
- codebase_breadth: 0 (only counts authored PRs)

Max possible score: 0 + 25 + 20 + 0 = 45. This is enough to appear in top 5 if their review quality is exceptional and others are spread thin. If this feels too low, codebase_breadth could also count reviewed PR file paths, boosting the max to 60.

### 8C. Very Large PRs (Generated Code, Migrations)

Some PRs have +10,000 lines but are auto-generated schema migrations or vendor file updates. The LLM prompt should include this instruction:

```
Note: High line counts do not automatically mean high complexity. 
Auto-generated migrations, vendor file updates, and schema dumps 
should be scored low on complexity and novelty even if they add 
thousands of lines.
```

### 8D. PR Descriptions That Are Empty

Some engineers write no PR description. The LLM still has the title, file paths, and diff stats. The prompt handles this:

```
If a PR description is missing, score based on the title, file paths, 
and diff statistics. Note in your reasoning that the assessment is 
based on limited information.
```

### 8E. Co-authored PRs

GitHub shows co-authors in the commit trailer. The GraphQL API surfaces the PR author only. For this 1.5-hour scope, attribute the PR to the merge author only. Note this limitation in methodology.

---

## Part 9: Execution Pipeline

### Step 1: Data Gathering (Python script, ~5 min runtime)
```
fetch_prs(repo="PostHog/posthog", days=90)
  -> for each page of 100 merged PRs:
       -> fetch PR metadata, files, reviews via GraphQL
       -> filter out bot authors
       -> save to raw_data.json
```

### Step 2: LLM Evaluation (Python script, ~5-10 min runtime)
```
load raw_data.json
batch PRs into groups of 6

# Phase 1: Score PRs
for each batch:
    -> send to Haiku with PR scoring prompt
    -> parse JSON response
    -> validate all fields present and scores in 1-10
    -> retry on parse errors (max 2 retries)
    
# Phase 2: Score Reviews (only non-trivial reviews)
filter reviews where body is not empty and not just "LGTM"
batch reviews into groups of 8
for each batch:
    -> send to Haiku with review scoring prompt
    -> parse, validate, retry

# Phase 3: Compute per-engineer aggregates
for each unique author:
    -> compute contribution_impact (normalized)
    -> compute review_impact (normalized, weighted by PR score)
    -> compute collaboration_velocity (from timestamps)
    -> compute codebase_breadth (from file paths)
    -> compute final_score
    -> select highlight PRs and reviews

# Phase 4: Output
rank engineers by final_score
take top 5
write scored_data.json (full structure from Part 7A)
```

### Step 3: Frontend Consumes scored_data.json
The Next.js app imports the JSON at build time. No runtime API calls. Instant loading.

---

## Part 10: Cost and Performance Estimates

| Item | Count | Model | Est. Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR scoring calls | ~170 batches (1000 PRs / 6) | Haiku | ~$0.80 |
| Review scoring calls | ~100 batches (non-trivial reviews) | Haiku | ~$0.40 |
| **Total** | **~270 LLM calls** | | **~$1.20** |

Wall-clock time with 10 concurrent requests: ~3-5 minutes for the LLM evaluation phase.

If using Sonnet instead: ~$6.00 total, ~10-15 minutes. Not justified for structured rubric scoring.

---

## Part 11: What Makes This Design Stand Out

1. **Every score is decomposable.** The engineering leader sees "87.3" and can click to see 40% from contributions (92.1), 25% from reviews (78.5), etc. Each of those decomposes further into individual PR scores with reasoning text.

2. **Every score is verifiable.** Every PR and review links to GitHub. The LLM's reasoning is shown alongside the score. The leader can spot-check in seconds.

3. **The formula is transparent.** The weights are documented. The methodology section explains everything in plain English.

4. **It rewards what actually matters.** An engineer who ships one transformative feature and reviews 20 PRs thoroughly outranks an engineer who makes 50 trivial commits. The scoring rubric makes this happen naturally, not through arbitrary filtering.

5. **Review-only contributors are recognized.** This is unusual and demonstrates that impact is not just about writing code.

6. **Collaboration velocity captures the "invisible" work.** Fast review turnaround is a force multiplier that traditional metrics miss entirely.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\Plan\First Prompt.md` - Contains the original architectural thinking and constraints that the evaluation layer must align with
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\Engineering Impact Dashboard Assignment.pdf` - The source requirements document defining evaluation criteria, red flags, and the "verifiability" constraint that drives the entire output schema design
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\CLAUDE.md` - Project conventions (Python default, minimal dependencies, working > perfect) that constrain implementation choices
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\.claude\settings.local.json` - Permitted toolchain (Python, pip, npm/pnpm, node) confirming the Python evaluator + Next.js frontend architecture is supported
- `D:\Projects\WorkWeave\.claude\skills\api\SKILL.md` - Pattern for building API-style services if the evaluator needs to expose results via an endpoint rather than static JSON
The Plan agent returned an excellent detailed design. Let me now write the plan file and ask you a few key design questions.

input.content:
# Engineering Impact Dashboard - Implementation Plan

## Context

**Problem:** Weave's coding assessment — analyze PostHog's GitHub repo (last 90 days) to identify the top 5 most impactful engineers. The key constraint: simple counts (LOC, commits, PRs) are NOT impact. They want creative, verifiable analysis. Single-page interactive dashboard deployed on Vercel.

**Core Insight:** Impact = the degree to which an engineer moves the product forward AND makes the team around them more effective. We decompose this into two pillars: *direct contribution quality* and *multiplier effect* (reviews, unblocking, collaboration).

---

## Architecture

```
Python Backend (backend/)
├── gather.py        # GitHub GraphQL API → raw_data.json
├── evaluate.py      # LLM scores each PR + review → scored dimensions
├── aggregate.py     # Compute per-engineer composites → results.json
└── data/
    ├── raw_data.json
    └── results.json  (consumed by frontend)

Next.js Frontend (frontend/)
├── Single page, reads results.json at build time
├── Top 5 engineer cards with pillar breakdowns
├── Drill-down to individual PR scores + GitHub links
└── Methodology section explaining the formula
```

---

## Part 1: Data Gathering (`gather.py`)

**Source:** GitHub GraphQL API on `PostHog/posthog`, last 90 days of merged PRs.

**Per PR, fetch via GraphQL:**
- title, body (truncated 1500 chars), author.login, avatar_url
- labels, mergedAt, createdAt
- additions, deletions, changedFiles
- files (first 50): path, additions, deletions
- reviews: author, state, body (truncated 500 chars), submittedAt, comment count
- comments.totalCount

**Bot filtering:** Exclude `dependabot`, `github-actions[bot]`, `posthog-bot`, any login containing `[bot]`.

**Output:** `raw_data.json` with all PR + review data. Cached so we don't re-fetch.

**Estimated API calls:** ~15-30 paginated GraphQL queries (100 PRs/page). Well within rate limits with a GitHub token.

---

## Part 2: LLM Evaluation (`evaluate.py`)

### PR Scoring — 5 Dimensions (each 1-10)

For each merged PR, the LLM scores:

| Dimension | What it measures | Low (1-2) | High (9-10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Complexity** | Technical difficulty | Typo, config tweak | New subsystem, complex algorithm |
| **Scope of Impact** | How broadly it affects the product | Internal/cosmetic | Core pipeline, all users |
| **Type Weight** | Inherent value of the work category | Dep bump, formatting | Feature, critical bugfix, security |
| **Risk & Judgment** | Engineering judgment required | Safe, isolated | Migration, data integrity |
| **Novelty** | New capability vs maintenance | Routine maintenance | Net-new capability |

**Type Classification** (one of): `feature`, `bugfix-critical`, `bugfix-minor`, `refactor`, `performance`, `tests`, `docs`, `config/infra`, `dependencies`

**LLM also returns:** `one_line_summary` + `reasoning` (2-3 sentences) — this is the verifiability layer.

**PR Composite:** `complexity*0.25 + scope*0.30 + type_weight*0.20 + risk*0.15 + novelty*0.10`

### Review Scoring — 3 Dimensions (each 1-10)

| Dimension | Low (1-2) | High (9-10) |
|---|---|---|
| **Review Depth** | "LGTM" / rubber stamp | Identifies architectural issues |
| **Issue Detection** | No issues raised | Caught bugs, security issues |
| **Constructiveness** | No actionable feedback | Problems + suggested solutions |

**Optimization:** Empty/LGTM-only reviews get default scores (depth:2, detection:1, constructive:1) without an LLM call.

**Review weighted by parent PR score:** `review_composite * (pr_score / 10)` — reviewing a high-impact PR deeply matters more.

### Batching Strategy
- PRs: batches of 6, ~170 calls for ~1000 PRs
- Reviews: batches of 8, ~100 calls for non-trivial reviews
- Model: Claude Haiku (fast, cheap, good for structured rubrics — ~$1-2 total)
- Structured JSON output, validated, retry on parse errors

### What the LLM Sees Per PR (no raw diffs)
- Title, description (truncated)
- File paths changed + per-file stats
- Labels
- Diff stats (additions/deletions)
- Review summaries

---

## Part 3: Aggregation (`aggregate.py`)

### Four Pillars Per Engineer

**1. Contribution Impact (40% of final score)**
```
sum(pr_composite_score for all authored PRs) → normalized 0-100
```
5 high-impact PRs (scores 8-9) outranks 20 trivial PRs (scores 1-2).

**2. Review Impact (25%)**
```
sum(weighted_review_score for all reviews given) → normalized 0-100
```
Recognizes engineers who accelerate others through deep reviews.

**3. Collaboration Velocity (20%)**
- Review turnaround: median time from review-request to review-submission
- Feedback responsiveness: median time from CHANGES_REQUESTED to next update
- Formula: `100 * (1 - clamp(median_hours / 48, 0, 1))`
- Pure timestamp math, no LLM needed.

**4. Codebase Breadth (15%)**
- Count distinct PostHog codebase zones touched: `frontend/`, `posthog/api/`, `posthog/models/`, `ee/clickhouse/`, `plugin-server/`, `posthog/tasks/`, etc.
- `unique_zones / total_zones * 100`
- Rewards full-stack contributors, but low weight so specialists aren't penalized.

**Final Score:** `contribution*0.40 + review*0.25 + velocity*0.20 + breadth*0.15` → 0-100

---

## Part 4: Frontend (Next.js + Tailwind)

### Single Page Layout
- **Header:** Title, date range, "How Scores Are Calculated" toggle
- **Top 5 Cards:** Each shows rank, avatar, name, final score, four pillar bars
- **Expand on click:** Top PRs with individual scores, one-line summaries, GitHub links
- **Review highlights:** Best reviews with reasoning
- **Methodology footer:** Collapsible section with formula + dimension definitions

### Verifiability (addresses red flags)
- Every score shows its components — no opaque numbers
- Every PR/review links to GitHub for manual spot-checking
- LLM reasoning displayed alongside scores
- Formula shown with tooltips
- Type badges (feature/bugfix/refactor) give instant context

### Data Loading
- `results.json` imported at build time (static). No API calls at runtime. Instant load.

---

## Part 5: File Structure

```
D:\Projects\WorkWeave\
├── backend/
│   ├── gather.py          # GitHub GraphQL data collection
│   ├── evaluate.py        # LLM scoring of PRs and reviews
│   ├── aggregate.py       # Per-engineer composite calculation
│   ├── main.py            # Orchestrator: gather → evaluate → aggregate
│   ├── requirements.txt   # httpx, anthropic
│   └── data/
│       ├── raw_data.json
│       └── results.json
├── frontend/
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── next.config.js
│   ├── tailwind.config.js
│   ├── app/
│   │   ├── layout.tsx
│   │   └── page.tsx       # Single page dashboard
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── EngineerCard.tsx
│   │   ├── PillarBreakdown.tsx
│   │   ├── PRDetail.tsx
│   │   └── Methodology.tsx
│   └── public/

# Engineering Impact Dashboard - Implementation Plan

## Context

**Problem:** Weave's coding assessment — analyze PostHog's GitHub repo (last 90 days) to identify the top 5 most impactful engineers. The key constraint: simple counts (LOC, commits, PRs) are NOT impact. They want creative, verifiable analysis. Single-page interactive dashboard deployed on Vercel.

**Core Insight:** Impact = the degree to which an engineer moves the product forward AND makes the team around them more effective. We decompose this into two pillars: *direct contribution quality* and *multiplier effect* (reviews, unblocking, collaboration).

---

## Architecture

```
Python Backend (backend/)
├── gather.py        # GitHub GraphQL API → raw_data.json
├── evaluate.py      # LLM scores each PR + review → scored dimensions
├── aggregate.py     # Compute per-engineer composites → results.json
└── data/
    ├── raw_data.json
    └── results.json  (consumed by frontend)

Next.js Frontend (frontend/)
├── Single page, reads results.json at build time
├── Top 5 engineer cards with pillar breakdowns
├── Drill-down to individual PR scores + GitHub links
└── Methodology section explaining the formula
```

---

## Part 1: Data Gathering (`gather.py`)

**Source:** GitHub GraphQL API on `PostHog/posthog`, last 90 days of merged PRs.

**Per PR, fetch via GraphQL:**
- title, body (truncated 1500 chars), author.login, avatar_url
- labels, mergedAt, createdAt
- additions, deletions, changedFiles
- files (first 50): path, additions, deletions
- reviews: author, state, body (truncated 500 chars), submittedAt, comment count
- comments.totalCount

**Bot filtering:** Exclude `dependabot`, `github-actions[bot]`, `posthog-bot`, any login containing `[bot]`.

**Output:** `raw_data.json` with all PR + review data. Cached so we don't re-fetch.

**Estimated API calls:** ~15-30 paginated GraphQL queries (100 PRs/page). Well within rate limits with a GitHub token.

---

## Part 2: LLM Evaluation (`evaluate.py`)

### PR Scoring — 5 Dimensions (each 1-10)

For each merged PR, the LLM scores:

| Dimension | What it measures | Low (1-2) | High (9-10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Complexity** | Technical difficulty | Typo, config tweak | New subsystem, complex algorithm |
| **Scope of Impact** | How broadly it affects the product | Internal/cosmetic | Core pipeline, all users |
| **Type Weight** | Inherent value of the work category | Dep bump, formatting | Feature, critical bugfix, security |
| **Risk & Judgment** | Engineering judgment required | Safe, isolated | Migration, data integrity |
| **Novelty** | New capability vs maintenance | Routine maintenance | Net-new capability |

**Type Classification** (one of): `feature`, `bugfix-critical`, `bugfix-minor`, `refactor`, `performance`, `tests`, `docs`, `config/infra`, `dependencies`

**LLM also returns:** `one_line_summary` + `reasoning` (2-3 sentences) — this is the verifiability layer.

**PR Composite:** `complexity*0.25 + scope*0.30 + type_weight*0.20 + risk*0.15 + novelty*0.10`

### Review Scoring — 3 Dimensions (each 1-10)

| Dimension | Low (1-2) | High (9-10) |
|---|---|---|
| **Review Depth** | "LGTM" / rubber stamp | Identifies architectural issues |
| **Issue Detection** | No issues raised | Caught bugs, security issues |
| **Constructiveness** | No actionable feedback | Problems + suggested solutions |

**Optimization:** Empty/LGTM-only reviews get default scores (depth:2, detection:1, constructive:1) without an LLM call.

**Review weighted by parent PR score:** `review_composite * (pr_score / 10)` — reviewing a high-impact PR deeply matters more.

### Batching Strategy
- PRs: batches of 6, ~170 calls for ~1000 PRs
- Reviews: batches of 8, ~100 calls for non-trivial reviews
- Model: Claude Haiku (fast, cheap, good for structured rubrics — ~$1-2 total)
- Structured JSON output, validated, retry on parse errors

### What the LLM Sees Per PR (no raw diffs)
- Title, description (truncated)
- File paths changed + per-file stats
- Labels
- Diff stats (additions/deletions)
- Review summaries

---

## Part 3: Aggregation (`aggregate.py`)

### Four Pillars Per Engineer

**1. Contribution Impact (40% of final score)**
```
sum(pr_composite_score for all authored PRs) → normalized 0-100
```
5 high-impact PRs (scores 8-9) outranks 20 trivial PRs (scores 1-2).

**2. Review Impact (25%)**
```
sum(weighted_review_score for all reviews given) → normalized 0-100
```
Recognizes engineers who accelerate others through deep reviews.

**3. Collaboration Velocity (20%)**
- Review turnaround: median time from review-request to review-submission
- Feedback responsiveness: median time from CHANGES_REQUESTED to next update
- Formula: `100 * (1 - clamp(median_hours / 48, 0, 1))`
- Pure timestamp math, no LLM needed.

**4. Codebase Breadth (15%)**
- Count distinct PostHog codebase zones touched: `frontend/`, `posthog/api/`, `posthog/models/`, `ee/clickhouse/`, `plugin-server/`, `posthog/tasks/`, etc.
- `unique_zones / total_zones * 100`
- Rewards full-stack contributors, but low weight so specialists aren't penalized.

**Final Score:** `contribution*0.40 + review*0.25 + velocity*0.20 + breadth*0.15` → 0-100

---

## Part 4: Frontend (Next.js + Tailwind)

### Single Page Layout
- **Header:** Title, date range, "How Scores Are Calculated" toggle
- **Top 5 Cards:** Each shows rank, avatar, name, final score, four pillar bars
- **Expand on click:** Top PRs with individual scores, one-line summaries, GitHub links
- **Review highlights:** Best reviews with reasoning
- **Methodology footer:** Collapsible section with formula + dimension definitions

### Verifiability (addresses red flags)
- Every score shows its components — no opaque numbers
- Every PR/review links to GitHub for manual spot-checking
- LLM reasoning displayed alongside scores
- Formula shown with tooltips
- Type badges (feature/bugfix/refactor) give instant context

### Data Loading
- `results.json` imported at build time (static). No API calls at runtime. Instant load.

---

## Part 5: File Structure

```
D:\Projects\WorkWeave\
├── backend/
│   ├── gather.py          # GitHub GraphQL data collection
│   ├── evaluate.py        # LLM scoring of PRs and reviews
│   ├── aggregate.py       # Per-engineer composite calculation
│   ├── main.py            # Orchestrator: gather → evaluate → aggregate
│   ├── requirements.txt   # httpx, anthropic
│   └── data/
│       ├── raw_data.json
│       └── results.json
├── frontend/
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── next.config.js
│   ├── tailwind.config.js
│   ├── app/
│   │   ├── layout.tsx
│   │   └── page.tsx       # Single page dashboard
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── EngineerCard.tsx
│   │   ├── PillarBreakdown.tsx
│   │   ├── PRDetail.tsx
│   │   └── Methodology.tsx
│   └── public/
│       └── results.json   # Copied from backend output
├── CLAUDE.md
└── Engineering Impact Dashboard Assignment.pdf
```

---

## Verification Plan

1. **Data gathering:** Run `gather.py`, confirm `raw_data.json` has 90+ days of PRs, bot accounts filtered
2. **LLM evaluation:** Run `evaluate.py`, spot-check 5-10 PRs — does complexity:8 on a feature PR make sense? Does a typo-fix get complexity:1?
3. **Aggregation:** Run `aggregate.py`, verify top 5 list — cross-check against GitHub activity manually
4. **Frontend:** `npm run dev`, confirm single-page loads instantly, all GitHub links work, drill-down shows PR details
5. **Deploy:** Push to Vercel, verify the hosted URL loads < 10s

---

## Open Questions

1. **GitHub Token:** Do you have a `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var set, or should I prompt for one?
2. **Anthropic API Key:** We'll use Claude Haiku for LLM scoring. Is your Anthropic key available as `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`?
3. **90 vs 30 days:** Your plan says 30 days, assignment says 90. Going with 90 to meet requirements.

questions.question: Do you have a GitHub personal access token available? We need one for the GitHub GraphQL API (5000 req/hr vs 60/hr without).

options.label: Yes, I have one
options.description: I have a GITHUB_TOKEN env var or can provide one

options.label: No, I need to create one
options.description: I'll need to generate a fine-grained PAT with read access to public repos

options.label: Use unauthenticated
options.description: Only 60 req/hr — will be very slow for PostHog's volume

questions.question: For LLM evaluation, which model should we use? Haiku is 10x cheaper and fast, Sonnet is more nuanced but slower.

options.label: Claude Haiku (Recommended)
options.description: Fast, ~$1-2 total cost. Good enough for structured scoring rubrics. ~3-5 min for 1000 PRs.

options.label: Claude Sonnet
options.description: More nuanced judgment, ~$6-10 total, ~10-15 min. Better if you want higher quality reasoning text.

options.label: Mix: Haiku + Sonnet
options.description: Haiku for bulk PR scoring, Sonnet for review scoring and top-PR reasoning refinement.

questions.question: The assignment says 90 days minimum but your plan mentioned 30 days. Which time range should we use?

options.label: 90 days (Recommended)
options.description: Meets assignment requirement. More data for better accuracy. PostHog has plenty of activity.

options.label: 30 days
options.description: Faster to collect but doesn't meet the 'at least 90 days' requirement in the assignment.

questions.question: For the frontend framework, do you want Next.js with Tailwind (your original plan) or something simpler?

options.label: Next.js + Tailwind (Recommended)
options.description: Your original choice. Easy Vercel deploy, static JSON import, clean styling.

options.label: Plain React + Vite
options.description: Simpler setup, still deploys to Vercel. Less boilerplate.

options.label: Streamlit
options.description: All-Python, fastest to build, but less polished UI and harder to deploy on Vercel.
